title: California Motorcycle Accident Lawyer | Frank Penney Injury Law
metaDescription: California motorcycle accident lawyer. We represent riders, bikers, and motorbike crash victims. $1B+ recovered. Free consultation 24/7. No fee unless we win.
heroSubheadline: Injured Riding? Insurance Bias Against Bikers Is Real — We Level the Playing Field.
whyText: Motorcycle accident victims often face insurance company bias — the assumption that the rider was reckless. Frank Penney Injury Law has 30+ years of experience representing bikers and motorbike crash victims across California, and we know how to push back hard against that bias.
citiesTitle: California Motorcycle Accident Lawyers Across Northern California
citiesLede: Frank Penney Injury Law represents motorcycle accident victims across all of Northern California. Whether you ride a sportbike, cruiser, dirt bike, or moped, we handle motorbike crash cases for riders in Sacramento, Roseville, Fairfield, Stockton, Modesto, Chico, and everywhere in between. Bike crash injuries — road rash, fractures, head and back trauma — are often more severe than car accident injuries, and insurance companies routinely undervalue rider claims. Our motorbike injury attorneys know the bias bikers face and how to overcome it.
serviceDescription: Representing California motorcycle accident victims with $1 Billion+ recovered. We handle motorbike crashes, bike collisions, rider injuries, and biker insurance disputes throughout Sacramento, Roseville, Fairfield, Stockton, Modesto, and Chico.

faq.q: Why do insurance companies undervalue motorcycle accident claims?
faq.a: Insurance adjusters often assume bikers are reckless and look for any reason to reduce or deny claims. Combined with the typically severe injuries from a motorbike crash, this bias means rider claims are routinely undervalued. Having an experienced motorcycle accident lawyer push back against the bias is one of the most important things you can do for your case.

faq.q: What if I wasn't wearing a helmet — can I still get compensation?
faq.a: California requires helmet use under Vehicle Code §27803, but failing to wear one doesn't eliminate your claim. It may reduce compensation for head and neck injuries under comparative negligence, but it doesn't affect compensation for unrelated injuries (broken legs, road rash, internal injuries). And if the other driver was clearly at fault for the bike crash itself, you still have a strong claim.

faq.q: How is California motorcycle lane-splitting law applied to accident claims?
faq.a: California is the only state that explicitly allows lane-splitting under Vehicle Code §21658.1. CHP guidelines suggest splitting at no more than 10 mph faster than surrounding traffic and not above 50 mph. Lane-splitting alone doesn't make a rider at fault, but speed and circumstances matter. We're well-versed in defending riders against unfair lane-splitting allegations.

faq.q: What are common injuries from a motorbike or bike crash?
faq.a: Riders are far more exposed than car occupants. Common motorcycle accident injuries include road rash, fractures (legs, arms, ribs), head and neck trauma, back injuries, internal injuries, and serious soft-tissue damage. Many require long-term rehabilitation. We work with medical experts to fully document the long-term impact on a rider's life and earning capacity.

faq.q: How much is a motorcycle accident case worth?
faq.a: Motorcycle accident cases often involve serious injuries and large medical bills, leading to higher settlements. Frank Penney Injury Law has recovered $4.5M+ on a single motorcycle accident case. Final value depends on the severity of injuries, lost wages, future medical care, pain and suffering, and the at-fault driver's coverage.

faq.q: Who is at fault in a motorcycle vs. car accident?
faq.a: Most car-vs-motorbike crashes are caused by the car driver — failure to yield, unsafe lane changes, distraction, or simply not seeing the rider. California's pure comparative negligence rule means even if a rider shares some fault, they can still recover (reduced by their percentage). We investigate aggressively to establish the car driver's negligence.

faq.q: How long do I have to file a motorcycle accident lawsuit in California?
faq.a: Generally two years from the date of the bike crash under California Code of Civil Procedure §335.1. Government-defendant cases (a city bus or government vehicle hitting a rider) require a claim within six months. Don't wait — physical evidence and witness memories deteriorate quickly.

faq.q: What if I'm a rider hit by a distracted or impaired driver?
faq.a: Distracted driving (texting, phone use) and DUI are leading causes of motorbike crashes. Phone records, dashcam footage, and BAC results can prove the other driver's fault and may also support claims for punitive damages. We subpoena these records aggressively in cases against impaired or distracted drivers.

faq.q: Can I get my bike repairs covered separately from injury compensation?
faq.a: Yes. Property damage (your motorcycle) is handled separately from your bodily injury claim. Many riders settle the property damage portion quickly through collision or the at-fault driver's property damage liability coverage, while we work the larger injury claim. Don't let an insurance adjuster bundle them — settling property damage shouldn't waive your injury claim.

faq.q: Why hire a lawyer who specifically handles motorbike crash cases?
faq.a: Motorcycle accident cases require specific expertise: lane-splitting law, helmet rules, biker bias from juries and insurers, and understanding of rider injury patterns. Frank Penney Injury Law has 30+ years of experience representing California riders, and we treat every motorcycle crash victim like family.
